title: Combination Skin Routine: Exactly What to Buy (and in What Order)
description: Oily T-zone but dry cheeks? Here's a simple, derm-friendly skincare routine for combination skin — the exact products and the order to use them, morning and night.
painQuote: I have no idea what products I need for my skin type!
intro: If your forehead and nose get shiny by lunchtime but your cheeks still feel tight and flaky, you have combination skin — the most confusing type to shop for, because most products are built for one extreme or the other.

The good news: you don't need a 12-step routine or a cabinet full of products. You need four things, used in the right order, that hydrate the dry areas without feeding oil in the T-zone. Here's exactly what that looks like.

sections.h2: The only 4 steps combination skin actually needs
body: Morning: a gentle cleanser, a lightweight treatment, an oil-free moisturizer, and sunscreen. Night: the same cleanser, a targeted treatment, and your moisturizer. That's it.
The trick with combination skin is texture, not richness — you want thin, fast-absorbing layers so the oily zones don't feel greasy, while still delivering enough water to the dry zones.
Skip harsh foaming washes that leave skin squeaky — that tight feeling is your barrier being stripped, which actually triggers more oil. A gentle, non-stripping cleanser keeps both zones balanced.
If your skin reacts easily, go fragrance-free.

sections.h2: Treatment: niacinamide by day, target your concern by night
body: Niacinamide is the perfect daytime pick for combination skin — it calms oil and visibly tightens pores without drying you out. At night, use a treatment matched to your main goal (breakouts, texture, or dark spots).
Introduce only one active at a time, and start every other night so your skin can adjust.
A lightweight, oil-free lotion hydrates the dry patches without clogging the oily ones. Finish every morning with sunscreen — unprotected sun exposure undoes everything else you're doing.
Not sure which products fit your exact skin? Take the 60-second quiz and we'll match a full routine to you.

faq.q: Should combination skin use a moisturizer?
faq.a: Yes — always. Skipping moisturizer makes the dry areas worse and pushes the oily areas to produce even more oil. Use a lightweight, oil-free formula.

faq.q: How long until I see results?
faq.a: Give any new routine 4–6 weeks of consistent use before judging it. Skin cell turnover takes time, and switching products too fast makes it hard to know what's working.

faq.q: Can I use the same products in summer and winter?
faq.a: You may want a slightly richer moisturizer in winter and a lighter one in summer, but your cleanser, treatments, and SPF can stay the same year-round.
